data.title: Big March Deals Push Total Outsize Transaction Value To Multiyear High In Q1
data.description:
There were 11 M&A deals worldwide with transaction values exceeding $10 billion in the first quarter. The period's total transaction value of $305.33 billion was the highest since the second quarter of 2019.

In March, there were four $10 billion-plus deals announced with a combined transaction value of $98.77 billion. Two of those deals ranked among the largest announced in the last 12 months, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence data.

In the largest deal of the month, an investor group on March 2 announced its plan to purchase Virginia-based power company AES Corp. (AES). The sale has a deal value of $10.82 billion and a transaction value of $48.08 billion, which includes the deal value plus the value of assumed current liabilities. Based on the transaction value, it ranks as the fifth-largest deal announced in the past 12 months.

The investor group purchasing the power company includes BlackRock Inc. (BLK) subsidiary Global Infrastructure Management LLC, which was also a buyer in the $40 billion acquisition of Aligned Data Centers LLC, announced in October 2025. The takeover addresses a growing need for power driven by AI.

Zurich-based insurance company Zurich Insurance Group AG (ZURVY) on March 2 announced a definitive agreement to acquire London-based insurer Beazley PLC (BZLEY)(BZLYF) after two failed attempts. That deal has a transaction value of $10.84 billion.

Zurich Insurance Group sent Beazley its first offer Jan. 4, then followed up Jan. 19 and again on Feb. 4. Beazley's management rejected initial bids for undervaluing the company, according to a bid announcement. The final offer gives Beazley's shareholders 1,310 pence in cash per Beazley share and a dividend of 25 pence per Beazley share.

In the US life insurance sector, Houston-based Corebridge Financial Inc. (CRBG) said March 26 that it plans to acquire New York City-based Equitable Holdings Inc. (EQH) in a deal with a transaction value of $10.77 billion. The merger will fold Equitable's specialty finance business into Corebridge's larger retirement and insurance business and create a top US life insurance group, according to Market Intelligence data.

In another Texas-based deal, Houston-based Sysco Corp. (SYY) said March 30 that it plans to acquire Whitestone, New York-based Jetro Cash & Carry LLC, the parent company of Restaurant Depot LLC. The food distribution deal has a transaction value of $29.08 billion.

Sysco and Jetro Cash & Carry have complementary products and can streamline supplier pricing to improve combined profitability, Sysco Chairman and CEO Kevin Hourican said during an M&A call with analysts.

About this analysis

This feature classifies M&A deals with transaction values of more than $10 billion by their definitive agreement date. It excludes deal announcements or announced bids that have not reached a definitive agreement. Installments of this feature before March 2026 included all announced bids.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Osterweis Capital Management Q2 2026 Strategic Income Outlook
data.description:
Despite a confluence of economic shocks in the first quarter, markets have held up remarkably well, but cracks appear to be forming beneath the surface.

Like the title from the Oscar-winning movie, Everything, Everywhere All at Once, the first three months of 2026 featured a mind-boggling confluence of geopolitical and economic events.

A partial list includes the arrest and extradition of Venezuela’s president to the United States, further threats regarding Greenland, IEEPA tariffs being overturned by the Supreme Court, AI breakthroughs that triggered massive declines in software equity valuations, continued problems in the private credit market, the four-year anniversary of the Russia/Ukraine conflict, an ongoing DOJ investigation into Fed Chair Jerome Powell, a partial government shutdown of DHS/TSA, and, as if all of that were not enough, a complex and disruptive war with Iran.

Still, despite the constant chaos, markets somehow held up relatively well in the first quarter. But beneath the surface, cracks appear to be forming. Markets can discount each event in isolation, but absorbing them all at once is another matter. Below we take a closer look at some of the most consequential moments and themes of 2026.

The operation in Venezuela appears to have been an unqualified military success, but thus far the economic impact has been negligible, and the longer-term prospects are far from certain. While Venezuela is reputed to have the world’s largest oil reserves, a combination of sanctions, corruption, and lack of investment has caused the amount of Venezuelan oil exported globally to decline rapidly over the last several years.

Despite the arrest of Maduro, moves to entice U.S. oil companies to upgrade Venezuelan drilling infrastructure have been met with an icy rebuff. Venezuela’s heavy sour crude is an ideal feedstock for many refineries on the U.S. Gulf Coast, so there clearly is potential to increase future exports.

However, estimates by Rystad Energy put the needed investment at $150 billion over the next 10-15 years, which has been a tough sell given the previous losses experienced by U.S. companies in Venezuela and concerns about policy instability. We believe it will be at least a few years before we see a material increase in oil production.

Likewise, although the Supreme Court’s reversal of the IEEPA tariffs was a landmark decision that affirmed the limits of presidential authority, it did little to change the reality on the ground. The first set of Trump tariffs was put in place just under a year ago, and companies have been adjusting and planning based on that framework.

If the ruling had its intended effect, we would already be seeing businesses revert to traditional supply chains, but that is not happening. Instead, across-the-board replacement tariffs were immediately announced by the Administration (though they may take longer to implement).

This prompted some countries and economic blocs to negotiate new agreements, while others have ceased negotiating until there is more clarity. Regardless, higher tariffs seem to be the new normal and are being priced in by the markets. Companies have been raising prices, and inflation will likely remain well above the Fed’s 2% target. We are keeping an eye out for any further changes.

AI has been another big headline maker over the last 12 months, particularly recently with further advancements in Anthropic’s Claude (ANTHRO) and OpenAI’s ChatGPT (OPENAI).

The revenue generated from LLMs to date pales in comparison to the enormous amount of capital being invested to develop them. The chart below shows the CapEx being spent by the major hyperscalers for data centers and the computing infrastructure needed to handle the forecasted growth of AI.

Anecdotally, we have not heard on earnings calls that these companies are achieving large economic returns to date. Granted it is early stages, but returns will need to justify these capital costs. The big concern for us is the funding of this CapEx, which originally relied on equity and free cash flow, but has morphed into massive piles of debt.

A significant amount of money has been raised in the investment grade and private credit markets, but billions of dollars have been issued in the convertible and high-yield markets as well. Companies like Meta (META) and Microsoft (MSFT) have recently engaged in large, bespoke arrangements with both public and private lenders, which brings new risks to the market that we have been carefully avoiding.

Separately, nobody yet knows whether AI will save money and generate profitable growth for businesses and/or how it will ultimately impact the labor force. Will there be massive layoffs and rising unemployment due to the replacement of workers, as Citrini Research and Dario Amodei, the founder of Anthropic, have hypothesized?

Or is it just another efficiency layer built on top of existing systems – a force multiplier that allows workers to be more efficient and increase productivity? Only time will tell, but the turmoil of day-to-day headlines is something investors have to parse.

A related topic that started making regular headlines late last year is the ongoing trouble in the private credit market. As the chart below shows, the asset class has grown extremely rapidly since its inception in 2002.

As you might expect, fast growth like this often leads to fast (read: careless) lending, which inevitably leads to credit problems. In fact, there have been many stories in the past several months about private credit borrowers negotiating liability management exercises – a.k.a. distressed exchanges – where lenders are taking the ownership keys from the sponsors due to missed payments or covenant breaches.

In some cases, these businesses are struggling badly enough that the new owners even write down their investments shortly after acquiring them. J.P. Morgan high-yield credit strategists estimate that from 2010 to 2023, 50% of distressed exchanges eventually defaulted again. Something to keep an eye on.

Industry concentration is another major risk in private credit. Matt Mish, a credit strategist at UBS, estimates that 24% of public Business Development Company (BDC) holdings are in technology and 30% are in business services.

For comparison, exposure in the traditional high-yield market is 7.3% and 5.7%, respectively. He believes both sectors are disproportionately susceptible to, at a minimum, the perception of AI disruption, and at worst, an existential threat.

Prior to the AI boom, software leveraged buyouts (LBOs) were easily able to obtain financing in the BDC, private credit, and leveraged loan markets (deliberately bypassing the high-yield market) because they were seen as safe borrowers due to their attractive business models, particularly high switching costs and expanding profit margins.

However, software companies are now seen as highly vulnerable because LLMs like Anthropic’s Claude can develop applications at nearly zero expense, rapidly eroding the competitive advantage that defined the industry for a generation. It is no wonder we have seen a significant uptick in nervous investors trying to withdraw capital from these less liquid vehicles.

Many non-publicly traded private credit funds have had to either limit quarterly withdrawals from investors or sell off pieces of their portfolio at discounts to meet redemptions.

UBS estimates private credit defaults could reach a high of 15% and leveraged loans 10%, which is significantly higher than the historical default rate for high yield bonds (typically 3%-5%). How long this takes to materialize is a big unknown, but we do know the bankruptcy cycle is often long-tailed.

However, we do not believe the troubles in BDCs and private credit funds are a systemic risk for the markets or the banking system, but rather an idiosyncratic risk for investors in those specific funds. One bright spot is that there is very little overlap between the borrowers in the private credit/leveraged loan market and the rest of the high-yield market.

The withdrawal demand from private credit investors and the forced selling may take several quarters to subside, and it may be several years before we know the true impact of AI disruption on the debt markets and overall economy. This is all part of the growing pains of a relatively new industry.

Finally, and most importantly, on February 28th, the United States and Israel attacked Iran, throwing more chaos into world markets and the global economy. After hitting all-time highs in January and February, the Dow Industrial and S&P Indices are now down on the year.

Oil is hovering around $100 per barrel as the Strait of Hormuz is essentially closed. About 20% of the world’s liquefied natural gas and 25% of the world’s oil originate in the region and must pass through the Strait.

In addition, about one-third of seaborne fertilizer for farming also travels through the strait. Depending on what crops they grow, farmers could be in planting season just as fertilizer prices are spiking, triggering higher food costs among other unintended consequences.

In times of global uncertainty, demand for U.S. Treasuries usually increases due to the so-called “flight to safety” trade, but during this war, Treasury yields have moved higher as worries about both inflation and excessive borrowing (because of the cost of the conflict) are reducing demand.

Pundits have now pivoted away from Fed cuts to the possibility of a rate hike. While it has only been a few weeks, we are already seeing reverberations. Damage to gas and oil infrastructure in neighboring Middle East producers, and subsequent shutdowns of production, could take a long time to recover as chemical plants and oil fields do not turn on overnight.

In fact, in some cases, it can take months, or longer, before they can return to full production capacity. United Airlines (UAL) recently announced a 5% cut in its flight schedule for the second and third quarters due to fuel costs, and the company is estimating that oil could rise to $175/barrel and not return to $100/barrel until the end of 2027. Since the war started, jet fuel prices have almost doubled.

War in the Middle East only adds to the list of concerns the Federal Reserve has to work through. January and February data showed the economy had been neither running too hot nor too cold – sort of a Goldilocks economy.

CPI for January and February was relatively benign and showed a minor slowing over the last 12 months but is expected to spike soon due to the war and rise in energy prices. Job openings appear to have risen in January versus December (contrary to the concerns about AI labor disruption) and were also higher than January 2025.

Average unemployment claims have been hovering around 210,000 and are not yet showing a longer-term pickup due to announced or anticipated layoffs. As the economists at High Frequency Economics commented, “Claims are well within the recent range over the last two years…There is nothing to worry about in this report.”

Payroll data has been slightly more volatile in the last few months, but when averaged out, it has been somewhat listless. Both pending home sales and the National Home Builders survey show slight improvements, while new home sales have been mostly flat. Balancing all this with the added Treasury financing for the war, as well as the existing federal deficits, will be a tough job for the Fed.

In summary, the combination of heightened economic and geopolitical uncertainty feels unprecedented to us, and we have been observing the markets for over three decades. Thus, we are sticking with our defensive posture until some of these crosscurrents resolve themselves. We continue to maintain a healthy amount of liquidity and are comfortable with our duration and portfolio positioning for the current environment.

As always, we thank you for your confidence in our management and we look forward to hearing from you.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Federal Deficit: TTM Interest Expense Exceeds $1T
data.description:
Federal Budget

The Federal Government publishes the spending and revenue numbers on a monthly basis. The charts and tables below give an in-depth review of the Federal Budget, showing where the money is coming from, where it is going to, and the surplus or deficit.

The government fiscal year closes at the end of September, but this analysis will look at the calendar year budget. As shown below, the last three months all saw deficits, with 5 of the last 6 months showing deficits of more than $100B.

Figure 1: Monthly Federal Budget

Looking at the average monthly deficit, January and February were both worse than average, whereas March was slightly better.

Figure 2: Current vs Historical

We can look at the quarter in aggregate using the Sankey diagram below that shows the very large $566B deficit (annualized to $2.3T)!

The big concern with the chart below is how much Interest Expense has moved up, making up 13.6% of all spending in Q1. This is now the second largest line item behind only Social Security. This is how debt spirals work. The deficit now makes up 31% of all spending, which means that the US borrows 1 in every 3 dollars it spends.

Figure 3: Quarterly Federal Budget Sankey

This latest quarter was slightly better than last Q2 and also better than Q4. That is a good trend, but the numbers are still so bad that it is hard to see any positivity in these numbers.

Figure 4: Quarterly Historical Deficit/Surplus for Previous Five Years

The next two charts show the quarterly revenue and costs broken down by expense type. You can see the impact of the tariff revenue below, reaching $90B in Q4 but dropping to $76B as they were struck down by the Supreme Court.

Figure 5: Quarterly Receipts

On the flip side, you can see that spending has been relatively flat over the last 8 quarters. While still very high, it’s good that it is not growing in large chunks.

Figure 6: Quarterly Outlays

Interest Expense has ballooned higher to $1T on a trailing twelve-month basis. This is deadly for the budget. $1T a year in just interest expense is a massive thorn in the side of the US government.

Figure 7: TTM Interest Expense

Historical Perspective

Zooming out and looking over the history of the budget back to 2015 shows a more complete picture. The change since Covid is quite dramatic. In 2019, the deficit was $1.02T. As of March last year, it was $2T. Ironically, the most recent TTM is smaller at a $1.6T deficit.

Figure 8: Trailing 12 Months (TTM)

The next two charts zoom in on the recent periods to show the change when compared to pre-Covid. These charts show spending and revenue on a trailing 12-month basis period over period. The last 12 months was a record revenue number, and so was spending, but it did not grow as fast.

The reason for the smaller deficit over the last year can be attributed to tariffs, higher income taxes, and higher Social Security. With stagnant wages, this is pinching people more and more.

Figure 9: Annual Federal Receipts

Figure 10: Annual Federal Expenses

Finally, we finish with a Sankey showing TTM Deficit was -$1.63T, which is a massive issue going forward, representing 23% of all government spending. The government cannot afford to keep printing such massive debt figures.

Figure 11: TTM Federal Budget Sankey

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: SkyWest Going Into Q1: Buy Thesis Still Looks Strong
data.description:
SkyWest (SKYW) heads into Q1 with a lot of momentum behind it. That backs up my earlier Buy call. Since my last check-in, the stock’s up just 0.68%, from $93.67 to $94.91, basically flat, and it’s still well off the 52-week high of $123.94 from August. The sideways trading since November might make you think about sitting on the fence, but when I go through what’s in the latest 10-K, the earnings call and the latest press releases, I see a different story. The fundamentals and the way the market’s pricing the stock aren’t lining up.

The main reason to stay bullish is the solid performance in both the numbers and the day-to-day business. For 2025, SkyWest brought in $4.1 billion in total operating revenue, up 15% from $3.5 billion last year. Profits grew even faster: net income jumped to $428.3 million, or $10.35 per diluted share, compared to $323 million and $7.77 for 2024. Block hours went up by 14.7% even though they had 1% fewer planes in service (487 now). So, they’re squeezing more out of every aircraft, thanks in part to a steadier pipeline of pilots and more available captains. That’s showing up as higher earnings per asset. Contract flying still makes up the bulk of business, but they’ve also pushed further into prorate and charter work, which grew by 33.5% in revenue and is giving them more ways to make money.

Cash And Guidance

Management keeps saying this rebound in the business isn’t just a blip. The Q4 call lays it out: pretax income hit $566 million for 2025, up 31% on a 15% jump in block hours. That tells you they’re getting a lot more out of every extra flight hour. EBITDA came in at $982 million, about $100 million better than last year. Free cash flow topped $400 million for 2025. They put that money to work: paid down $492 million in debt, part of a 10% reduction in debt since the end of 2024, from $2.7 billion to $2.4 billion, and bought back $85 million in shares, double the pace from last year. Leverage at the end of the year dropped to the lowest point in a decade. Cash fell to $707 million from $802 million, but that was partly because they spent big on new equipment, debt repayment, and buybacks, not because something’s wrong in the business. For 2026, they’re planning CapEx in the $600-$625 million range, about flat with 2025. Management says block hours should grow in the mid-single-digit range and earnings per share for 2026 should land “in the mid-$11 area,” even though they expect seasonality to get a bit sharper, more like it was before COVID.

Contract Visibility

SkyWest locked in major capacity purchase agreements with United and Delta, which means it has no major E175 contract expirations until at least late 2028. Management’s words: “We now have no contract expirations on E175s until the back half of 2028.” They’ve also set up their fleet planning so they can be flexible. Of the 69 E175s they’ve ordered from Embraer, 25 are allocated to major partners, leaving 44 unassigned, which leaves them with a lot of flexibility to add or pause deliveries depending on what’s needed. At the same time, they’re moving parked dual-class CRJs back into service and using the rest of the fleet harder, which should mean more block hours if routes that have been closed off start coming back or growing. Leadership sounds sure of themselves: “We believe that we are positioned to drive long-term total shareholder returns by deploying our strong balance sheet and free cash flow generation against a variety of accretive opportunities.”

Labor And Risk

Labor costs are a big swing factor here. In 2025, salaries, wages, and benefits made up 45.3% of total operating expenses. More jumps in those would hit margins unless they can boost rates or volumes to make up for it. More regulation could pile on, too. Something like stricter pilot duty rules, or if a big partner runs into real trouble, could throw off their stable block hour setup and mess with their long-term contract model. If block hours stop growing or partners start pulling back, their operating leverage could flip the wrong way. Big picture risks aren’t off the table, either. Sudden government funding cuts for air traffic or more wild swings in interest rates would both drag on performance, although their CPA setup does shield them from fuel prices.

Valuation

Price-wise, the stock still looks cheap. Shares are going for about 9.1x trailing GAAP earnings, 8.3x forward, with an EV/EBITDA of 5.7 and price/book at 1.4. For a company growing earnings double digits, throwing off a lot of free cash, and with short-term flying contracts pretty much locked in, those are tight numbers. If investors start believing the earnings are repeatable and the company keeps returning capital, you could see the multiple move up. They’ve got $265 million in deferred revenue waiting to get recognized, which should help reported numbers over future periods, with management indicating roughly $20-$25 million per quarter. Over the last year, the stock hasn’t kept up with how well the business is actually doing. The stock remains below its August high despite stronger 2025 operating and earnings results. Both analysts and investors are still pretty cautious, even though Q2, Q3, and Q4 EPS of $2.91, $2.81, and $2.21 all blew past expectations.

Balance Sheet Progress

Some folks might say credit reserves going up, maintenance costs running high, or maybe stretching too far with regional upgrades are all warning signs. Those things are real, but right now, they just look like normal bumps in the road, not something that’ll wreck the business. Debt has declined by $1 billion since 2022, even with lots of spending on planes, and the company’s leverage looks better than it has in years. If block hours suddenly fall off or the labor market flips, that would mean a downgrade, because the company needs steady operations and solid partners to keep its safety net. People who are only watching short-term trends or travel swings are missing the bigger shift: SkyWest has better contracts now and tighter control over spending, which should help it do well whether the economy is running hot or not.

What To Watch

Looking ahead, the main thing is seeing real proof: block hour growth staying up in the mid-single digits, more deferred revenue coming in and clear signs that wage costs aren’t running away or are being passed through in the next round of CPA talks. If a partner’s credit gets shaky, new rules show up, or charter/prorate volumes start dropping, that would break the case. On the flip side, if wage pressures ease or regional air traffic comes back stronger than expected, estimates and valuations would probably move higher.

After the dip from earlier highs and how the stock hasn’t really moved even though the company’s been delivering, it looks like the market’s missing something. The mix of steady contracts, careful spending, and real efficiency improvements backs up a Buy call.

data.title: Amazon: Zoox Enters The Robo Taxi Race, Jassy On Growth And Anthropic Mythos
data.description:
My Las Vegas Zoox experience

Amazon (AMZN) is a stock I keep adding to, even though it's stuck in a pattern between $200 and $250 without a lot of indication that it can break out of that range anytime soon. This is fine by me, and Amazon is one of the long-term holdings that I anticipate only adding to with too many catalysts to count. Amongst the hyperscalers in the Mag 7, Amazon may get most of the profit from cloud computing, but the low-margin, high-volume retail business is unique amongst the group. This retail component makes me believe that Amazon is the most likely to be able to grow the top line out of the group, regardless of the world economy being in recession or expansion.

Amazon is also one of the top spenders on Research and Development and investment activities [on the cash flow statement], which can also include acquisitions.

A 2020 acquisition, Zoox, was a product I finally was able to experience.

Amazon Owns 100% of This Company

First off, Amazon owns all of Zoox, which was purchased in 2020 for $1.2 Billion.

With a lot of excitement around Tesla's (TSLA) robo-taxi business and Google's (GOOG)(GOOGL) Waymo, I'm surprised Zoox doesn't get more hype. The first public rides started in Las Vegas in 2025, and we were finally able to try one this spring break.

My Impressions

First off, this thing is unique in the US robo-taxi world. It has no steering wheel and 4 facing seats, two at each end of the vehicle.

Each individual gets a mini tablet that can scroll through Pandora music playlists and adjust the climate control. I was able to see this thing in action both in the daytime and at night.

The current route that the Zoox traverses in Las Vegas just goes from one end of Las Vegas Boulevard to the other, with several stops at many of the prized properties along the strip. We waited for the first ride after downloading the Uber-esque app at the Wynn for nearly an hour as we watched excited riders shuffle in and shuffle out of a never-ending stream of arriving Zoox. Finally, ours arrived around 10 PM, and the 5 of us got in. The roof at night is illuminated with stars, similar to a Rolls-Royce Ghost or Phantom.

Then, as the 5th person sat in the middle to one side of the car, a human got on the intercom, "Sorry, only 4 riders". We were so distraught at that moment and hoofed it back to our hotel. My kid was still determined to try it, and we left the parents at the hotel the next day to make sure we'd all fit in the cab.

When you get close to the cab, you simply press an "open door" button as the cab senses your phone. Everyone gets in, fastens their seatbelt, and then presses go on the tablet to take them to their predetermined destination. This ride was smooth and comfortable, and I would almost say it's a must-do experience if you are going to Vegas any time soon. I can also imagine a product like this being used in a convoy line in place of trains. If there's one thing the US has a near-impossible time doing, it's building railway tracks and subways. We could simply build carpool-type lanes on the freeways, maybe even with walls, made exclusively for robo-taxis like Zoox.

Jassy on Growth Story

CEO Andy Jassy also recently came out with a really good letter on the company's growth and direction. In addition to clearly emphasizing the growth rates in AWS, and the cost reduction to users by implementing Trainium chips, here are a few more highlights I enjoyed as a shareholder:

While we continue to work on productivity and inventory levels, robotics provides a step-level change for how we can deliver faster, reduce the cost of carrying more selection, and automate movements that cause strains and injuries to our teammates. Accelerated by acquiring Kiva in 2012, and investing in numerous robotics initiatives the last 14 years, we now have over one million robots operating in fulfillment centers helping with stowing, picking, sorting, and intra-facility transport.

Many don't realize the level of automation that Amazon warehouses are already going through. That quickly delivered package is not getting there that fast by chance.

Customer response has been overwhelmingly positive, with the average number of monthly Same-Day customers in rural areas nearly doubling in 2025 compared to the prior year. Once this expansion is complete, our network will be able to deliver over a billion more packages each year to customers living in over 13,000 zip codes spanning 1,200,000 square miles.

There is no other company making this level of effort to serve every community. The US will always be a mix of rural and urban, and Amazon is attacking this.

Over the last seven years, we’ve built a low Earth orbit satellite network (Amazon Leo) and put more than 200 satellites into space (which is the third-largest low Earth orbit network operating today).

Many think Starlink is the only game in town when it comes to satellite internet. They are more or less just the first to market. Amazon and others are coming.

Most recently, Delta Airlines, the highest grossing airline in the world, has announced it’s chosen Amazon Leo for its future Wi-Fi, and will begin with 500 planes in 2028. They join other Leo customers like JetBlue, AT&T, Vodafone, DIRECTV Latin America, Australia’s National Broadband Network, NASA, and others.

The airlines were typically the customers of Viasat, Inc. (VSAT). There will be many new choices of in-flight internet service in the next couple of years, including Amazon's LEO.

It’s hard to overstate my optimism for what’s ahead.

Our retail business is now approaching $600 billion in topline, yet roughly 80% of global retail sales still happens in physical stores. That will change.

AWS is at a $142 billion revenue run rate, and yet 85% of global IT spend remains on-premises. This will change.

Great way for Jassy to wrap this up. While we assume that these hyperscalers are reaching the apex of their total addressable market, they are just scratching the surface in this physical-to-digital shopping and compute shift.

Anthropic and Mythos

Amazon originally invested $4 billion in Anthropic, a roughly 10-20% minority stake plus a deep partnership. Similar to Microsoft and OpenAI, we have to assume that one day these private companies will go public and the stake will get a visible market value.

Claude and Anthropic seem to now be pulling away in the LLM AI space. Especially in regard to coding. Now the company is touting a new internal model, too dangerous to share with the public. Mythos is rumored to be able to find security flaws in the code of almost any software or operating system in existence, and a malicious operator might be able to create a cybersecurity panic.

Anthropic said they are only sharing Mythos with trusted partners currently. AWS is one of those. We have to imagine that cybersecurity firms could one day be at the mercy of the most powerful LLM model owners that can find and exploit or patch vulnerabilities faster and better than any of them currently can. Amazon and Google, in my opinion [Google acquired WIZ cybersecurity], could have competitive cybersecurity businesses in the future to the existing group.

Value of Amazon Anthropic Stake?

Anthropic could be up to a $30 Billion run rate by some estimates and growing. I already know several people who pay for Claude, and it is quickly becoming a required tool for software coders. I pay for it myself as well, but for legal document editing, more than anything, I've found the Claude Opus models to be the most powerful.

$30 Billion could quickly grow, and before the company goes public, it may be valued at half a trillion or more, similar to what's being talked about with OpenAI or SpaceX. That could already make the Amazon stake worth an estimated $60 billion on paper.

Valuation

I've noted in previous articles that under the hood, profitability would soon be revealed in the hyperscalers if they begin spending more on CAPEX than R&D. Research and development is an operating expense that affects GAAP income now but still helps grow revenue, a hidden expense akin to retained earnings to help the business grow. Now Amazon, Microsoft (MSFT), Google, and others have increased capital expenditures rather than soft costs like R&D. These are capitalized and not expensed immediately. Hence, we may see a spurt in GAAP income increases in the near term until the depreciation expense on data centers starts kicking in.

This is simply now more of a delay and smoothing of expenses rather than immediate deductions. This is just my running thesis that the earnings were always there, just not visible on the surface.

We can see the P/E ratio of Amazon dropping consistently over time. Investors are now pricing Amazon more realistically, and in turn, Amazon continues to grow profitability as the share price remains in a static pattern. In my opinion, this is a situation that is preferable to accumulate in. Contracting multiples but better forward numbers.

Similar pattern on the EV/EBITDA ratio, adding back non-cash expense. Amazon is trading now at just under 17x TTM.

Compared to the rest of the Mag 7 on an EV/EBITDA basis, the company looks fairly priced amongst the cohort. On a forward rather than TTM basis, Amazon only has a lower ratio/multiple than Meta (META):

If I simply use the average EV/EBITDA for the group excluding Tesla [which doesn't trade on fundamentals], we get 16.13x FWD. With TTM EBITDA at $145.7 Billion, the company has been growing EBITDA at a 25% CAGR since 2021 based on these numbers:

If 2026 gets us another 25% year, that would mean $145,731 X 1.25 = 182,163.75 possible FY 2026 EBITDA [in millions of USD].

Amazon also has had a 1.34% dilution CAGR since 2021. That would mean 10,734.9 X 1.0134 = 10,878.8 possible shares outstanding at the end of 2026.

$182,163 [EBITDA 26 estimate]/10,878.8 [shares outstanding estimate 26] = $16.74 EBITDA/share.

$16.74 X the average EV/EBITDA multiple of the Mag 7 ex Tesla of 16.13 X = $16.74 X 16.13 = $270.02 fair value.

Compared to Wall St. Ratings:

My fair value estimate falls just below the average analyst estimate in this scenario.

Growth Trends

Observing the 5-year pattern on Amazon of price growth vs. GAAP diluted EPS and revenue, the company is now trading price-wise below these metrics:

Balance Sheet

Amazon has an excellent balance sheet in my opinion, regardless of the market gripes over hyperscaler borrowing to build data centers. The company has plenty of borrowing capacity.

Balance sheet item Billions USD
Total Debt $157.3B
Cash & Short-Term Investments [subtract from LT debt] $123.3B
Net Debt $34.0B
EBITDA $150.4B
Net Debt / EBITDA 0.23x

With many companies being able to borrow up to 3 X net debt to EBITDA, Amazon has barely scratched the surface of what it can borrow.

The one negative on the balance sheet is the rising share float. Amazon continues to slowly dilute to supplement its balance sheet cash for investment and share-based compensation, which hurts forward EPS growth.

Risks

CEO Andy Jassy is enthusiastic about the demand and supply for AI being out of balance, with more demand than supply. I tend to believe this narrative just by parsing through headlines and data. The main issue, in my opinion, is electricity. Energy is a bottleneck that needs to be solved to continue this massive buildout. There could be some sunk costs that go into stalled projects that just don't end up having the power supply to turn them on. This is the biggest risk, in my opinion, when buying companies investing heavily into data centers.

Summary

Amazon has not been a great investment over the past 5 years, failing to match the market.

I do continue to believe that Amazon is possibly the most important company in the US economy. They are moving into many sectors and sub-sectors at once and dominating in many. Be it data centers, shipping and logistics, groceries, e-commerce, used car sales, pharmaceuticals, or now robo-taxis, Amazon dares to enter every market in one way or another.

They are also producing AI chips with the Trainium offering. Sooner or later, Amazon might become a self-sustaining ecosystem that can supply itself with almost everything it needs as well as supply the consumer. This long consolidation pattern is a good time to accumulate, in my opinion, if you have a long time horizon.

data.title: ASML: Defendable Growth In A Volatile Market With Upside Still Underestimated
data.description:
Investment Thesis

We believe ASML (ASML) is a key beneficiary of the strong AI-driven capex cycle. Continued advancement of process nodes and the rapid expansion in memory demand are extending the upcycle for semiconductor equipment. My analysis suggests that the scale of the order pipeline from memory producers is underestimated and ASML will operate at full capacity over the next two years. We still see upside for its valuation; therefore, we rate ASML as a Buy with a target price of $1,680. The primary risk is rising inflation across the AI supply chain, which may dilute real capex growth.

Company Overview: Technology Leadership Reinforced by a Strong Ecosystem

ASML is the only supplier of extreme ultraviolet (EUV) lithography systems globally, while also maintaining a dominant position in deep ultraviolet (DUV) lithography. Their equipment is essential for both logic and memory chip production, with customers including TSMC, Intel, Samsung, SK hynix, Micron, and others. ASML launched its first immersion DUV system in 2004 and EUV in 2016. Sales of DUV reached 12 billion euros in 2025, representing over 90% market share. Sales of EUV reached 11.7 billion euros and continue to grow rapidly. We believe ASML's monopoly position will remain solid for the foreseeable future, as the development of EUV required decades of effort and billions of dollars in R&D, making it difficult for competitors to catch up.

Strong capability in supply chain integration and alignment with customers widened its moat. Through equity investment and acquisitions, ASML has built deep relationships with both suppliers and customers. It acquired Brion, Cymer, HMI, and 24.9% of Zeiss in the past two decades. In particular, the integration of Cymer and Zeiss gives ASML access to leading light source and optics technology, limiting competitors from entering the high-end market. At the same time, key customers, including TSMC, Intel, and Samsung, were also early investors, sharing the risk and benefits of product development.

Through technological leadership in EUV, deep supply chain integration, and strategic alignment with a concentrated customer base, ASML has built a strong ecosystem that significantly raises the entry barriers.

Semi Equipment Upcycle is Expected to Extend to 2027

The global semiconductor equipment upcycle is expected to extend beyond 2027, fueled by robust chip demand, both logic and memory. Our core thesis is based on the strong expansion of hyperscaler capex and the accelerating adoption of EUV lithography across both segments.

Shift in product mix driven by migration to smaller process nodes. DUV is mostly used for 28 to 7nm nodes, while EUV, primarily NXE Systems (low NA), is for 7 to 3 nm production, and EXE systems (high NA) are designed for 2 nm nodes. Currently, most EUV shipments are low NA, with Intel and Samsung having adopted a small number of high NA EUV. Although their largest customer, TSMC, has indicated that it will not use high NA for 2nm, A16, and A14, as multi-patterning is more cost-effective, ASML expects costs to converge for logic chip production using multi-patterning and single exposure in 2028, with high NA likely more cost-effective beyond that. This transition occurs earlier for DRAM manufacturing in 2025. Based on this, the market broadly expects TSMC to introduce high-NA EUV beyond 2028.

Given the much higher price of high NA EUV, around 400 million euros, compared to 230 million euros for low NA and 43 million for DUV, the ongoing shift toward advanced nodes will structurally increase ASML's revenue mix and margins. In the past decade, TSMC has moved from a 20nm process to 2nm, accompanied by increasing usage of EUV. Today the AI-related demand is primarily concentrated in advanced nodes below 7 nm, so we expect sales of EUV to contribute more than 50% starting in 2026.

Furthermore, ASML is expected to increase the power of the light source in EUV to 1,000 watts from the current 600 watts, enabling chipmakers to produce up to 50% more chips per machine by 2030. We view this as a big move for ASML to strengthen its monopoly position by incentivizing customers to shift from DUV to EUV even as competitors attempt to challenge its position, particularly in lower‑end products such as DUV.

Key beneficiary of capex expansion. Based on the announcement, we estimate that capex by hyperscalers, including Amazon, Google, Microsoft, Meta, Oracle, and CoreWeave, will increase from $430 billion to around $720 billion in 2026, up 67% yoy. This translates into robust demand for high-performance logic and memory chips. The two largest logic chip makers: Intel plans to keep its capex flat, while TSMC guided for $56 billion in 2026, up 36% yoy. We estimate demand for EUV from TSMC and Intel is roughly 30 to 35 and 50 to 54 units in 2026 and 2027, respectively. In recent years, memory chip producers have increasingly adopted EUV to boost productivity due to robust AI demand. My analysis suggests that EUV demand from TSMC and Intel will reach 30 to 35 units in 2026 and 50 to 54 units in 2027, representing around 50% growth.

Adoption of EUV in the memory segment accelerated the EUV growth. Memory chip production is less complicated; for cost considerations, chip producers usually relied on DUV for DRAM production in the past. However, persistent memory shortages and soaring prices incentivize memory makers to use more EUV to boost productivity, particularly as they transition to more advanced process nodes such as 1c and 1d. For example, SK hynix and Micron started to adopt EUV in 2022 and 2024, respectively. SK hynix was reported to have placed an order of 6.9 billion euros with ASML in March, which translates to around 30 units of EUV based on my estimates. Samsung, the global leader in memory with 20% more capacity than SK hynix, expanding at roughly the same pace as we estimate, is expected to place similar orders. Micron, with roughly half the memory capacity, is also on track to scale EUV adoption.

In summary, this amount of orders is likely to exceed ASML's capacity even as ASML plans to expand capacity to 90 low NA EUV and 20 high NA EUV by 2027. The robust order pipeline will push the semiconductor equipment upcycle to 2027 in our view. We also see emerging demand for DUV from the Chinese memory producers. Changxin Memory (CXCC) is planning an IPO in 2H26. We expect it to invest heavily in DUV systems post-financing. This further supports our view that the upcycle will remain intact in the next two years.

Solid Financial Position

One of the advantages of investing in semiconductor equipment companies is high sales visibility. ASML has maintained strong growth from 2016 to 2025 with a revenue CAGR of 19%. This growth has increasingly been driven by its product upgrades, mainly EUV systems and immersion DUV (ArFi). In 2025, ASML reported new orders grew 48% yoy, implying that we're still in the middle of an upcycle. Also taking into account the capex guidance from customers, we expect ASML to operate at full capacity in the next two years with sales growing 17%/30% yoy in 2026 and 2027, respectively. Another advantage is its highly disciplined cost structure, with operating leverage that still has room for further improvement as EUV scales. Therefore, we estimate earnings to grow 22% and 35%, respectively, for the same period; thanks to an improving product mix, earnings growth becomes more resilient despite the cyclicity.

The company has guided gross margin to a range of 51% to 53% in 2026 and a long-term target of 54% to 60% by 2030. We view this target as achievable, supported by increasing sales of high-margin products. Although the sales of high-NA EUV systems may temporarily dilute margins due to low volumes. We expect this to be offset by improving economies of scale over time.

We also want to highlight ASML's cash management. Even with a long product delivery cycle, around 18 months, ASML receives payments based on production progress. This significantly reduces its financial risks and allows ASML to maintain a strong net cash position, which reached 9.6 billion euros in 2025. Free cash flow grew 22% in the same year, largely in line with earnings growth. Looking ahead, we expect free cash flow to moderate temporarily in 2026, mainly due to capacity expansion. As utilization remains high, we expect cash generation to accelerate, further strengthening its financial position.

Cycle Not Yet Peaked, Valuation Not Demanding

The semiconductor equipment industry is cyclical, with a typical cycle lasting around ten quarters. The current upcycle started in June 2025, and we believe it has not yet peaked. There are usually clear signals when a cycle peaks, such as weak new order growth and very conservative guidance, etc. In contrast, we have seen very strong order growth in 4Q25, and we continue to expect strong order momentum in 1Q26 due to memory and ASIC expansion. In our view, this upcycle may be longer than before due to the unprecedented AI demand, which is not yet fully priced in by the market. Compared with prior cycles, which were primarily driven by consumer electronics, which are more cyclical.

ASML is currently trading at 37.5x adjusted non-GAAP earnings, down from the recent high of 40.5x. We forecast diluted non-GAAP EPS to reach $35 and $48 in 2026 and 2027, respectively. Applying a 35x 2027 adjusted forward P/E, with a slight premium to the industry average, to reflect its strong growth prospects and market position, we derive a target price of $1,680. Improving order visibility and capacity guidance for 2027 and beyond or an upward revision to 2026 guidance in upcoming results announcements could serve as key catalysts.

Key Risks

The major risks that could challenge our investment thesis are the quality and sustainability of capex growth and potential technology disruption.

The upcycle is heavily supported by aggressive spending from hyperscalers, which is expected to grow 67% yoy in 2026. While due to inflation, the real capex growth may be overestimated. Google, for example, capex is expected to double to $180 billion in 2026, potentially $190 billion, while costs across the supply chain have also increased significantly; in particular, memory chip prices have doubled. Adjusting for inflation, we estimate the real capex increase is around 30%. If inflation continues to rise, the same amount of capex may translate to less demand for semiconductor equipment.

ASML currently trades at a premium relative to peers such as Applied Materials, Lam Research, and KLA Corporation, reflecting its monopoly position in EUV. It raises concerns over any changes in growth expectations or technology breakthroughs from competitors.

Conclusion

We consider the semiconductor equipment stocks to offer both defensibility and growth quality in a volatile market due to their high order visibility. Particularly, the potential order growth is not fully priced in, while the cyclical risk may be overestimated. Companies like ASML, Lam Research, and Applied Materials, etc., benefit from the robust memory demand, with Lam Research having greater exposure to DRAM. Meanwhile, we have seen consistent strong financial performance for these names across cycles, which makes them attractive to own. Therefore, we initiate ASML with a Buy rating.

data.title: Niagen: High-Quality Growth At A Fair Price
data.description:
Niagen Bioscience (NAGE) is a high-growth, high-profitability firm that may catch the eye of many growth-focussed investors. In fiscal 2025, they grew their net sales by 30%, they expanded their profitability by 2.5 percentage points, and they more than doubled their net income. At the same time, their debt remains at less than $3 million, compared to roughly $65 million cash on hand. The firm appears fairly valued currently, and the lack of insider selling activity also gives confidence in both the outlook and the valuation. For these reasons, I initiate coverage on NAGE with a buy rating.

Financial Results

Fiscal year 2025 delivered strong results. They achieved a revenue of $129.4 million, representing a 30% growth compared to the prior year. It is not simply the growth I like here, but the robustness of the growth. Each product category improved significantly and contributed to his growth.

It's important to mention that the firm achieved these results by not compromising on profitability. In fiscal year 2025, the gross profit margin reached 64.3%, 2.5% higher than in the previous year, and net income more than doubled.

Further, the firm appears also attractive from a capital structure point of view. NAGE has more than $64 million cash on hand, in contrast to the $2.8 million debt. This gives the firm meaningful financial flexibility, even in times of market distress.

Looking forward, we can also be optimistic about what NAGE forecasts. While sales growth is expected to slow in fiscal 2026, it is anticipated to remain in the double-digit region. E-commerce and recurring revenues from established partnerships are foreseen to drive this growth. The gross margin is also expected to improve, mainly due to a more favourable product mix and lower cost inventory sales.

All in all, I find Niagen's growth, operational excellence, and capital structure attractive. I think from a fundamental point of view, NAGE could be an interesting investment for growth-focussed investors.

Macro Landscape

Despite the strong top line growth, we have to keep the macroeconomic circumstances in mind. Consumer confidence in the United States - and globally - is very poor, meaning that people are less and less certain about their financial stability and about the health of the economy overall. Niagen sells personal care products, including dietary supplement products, and commercializes food-grade and pharmaceutical-grade ingredient technologies. In my view, many of these products cannot be considered as essentials, and with poor sentiment, many people may cut the budget on such products. This could be a meaningful risk for NAGE at the current point in time.

On a more positive note, however, Niagen announced a partnership with Truemed to expand payment flexibility, which may help keep demand steady or even boost it.

Further, the elevated energy prices and the failure to reach a deal with Iran over the weekend put upward pressure on the costs. High oil prices generally lead to elevated transportation and manufacturing costs that could eat into the firm's profitability if they are not able to pass on the expenses to their customers. Due to the inflationary effects of high oil prices, interest rates could also remain higher for longer, creating significant headwinds for companies with significant debt in their capital structure. This is, however, not the case for NAGE.

Macroeconomic risks remain important factors to consider. I believe these risks, however, are well reflected in the firm's 2026 guidance, and I also do not expect their impact to be more severe than the firm anticipates.

Valuation

Looking at a set of traditional price multiples, one may get the impression that Niagen is expensive. If we look at the metrics compared to the sector median, it indeed appears so, but when we compare to the firm's own historic averages, all metrics indicate a discount.

If we look at these metrics in absolute terms and put the broad-based, robust growth that the firm keeps on delivering, I believe that the valuation is currently justified for a growth stock. Looking at the insider activity, we can also get optimistic. Although there were no insider buys in 2026, there were also no sells, and the three most recent activities were all buys.

Last, but not least, I also have to mention that NAGE issued a significant amount of shares in the past 5 years. The rate at which new shares are issued slowed, and I expect it to further slow as the company is now profitable and able to generate cash from its operations to finance further growth opportunities.

For these reasons, I believe that Niagen is fairly valued right now.

Conclusions

I find Niagen's business attractive from a growth and fundamental point of view. In fiscal 2025, the company increased its sales by 30%, improved its profitability metrics, and net income roughly doubled compared to fiscal 2024.

From a capital structure perspective, the firm is also attractive, as they have very little debt and a much higher amount of cash on hand, which allows for financial flexibility.

Macroeconomic headwinds exist - specifically low consumer confidence and elevated energy prices - but considering the strong demand and high sales growth, I believe that NAGE will be able to withstand these headwinds.

The stock appears to be fairly valued, and the lack of insider selling gives even more confidence in both the valuation and outlook.

For these reasons, I assign NAGE a buy rating.

data.title: Bilibili: Stock's Risk/Reward Is Completely Different From Last Year
data.description:
Thesis

Bilibili's (BILI) release of earnings for Q3 and Q4 reflects some stagnation of the business, particularly for gaming and VAS. Progress on gaming was a major catalyst that fueled the stock's outperformance in 2024 and 2025, being a powerful driver for sales growth and margin expansion. That driver has now been called into question. This puts Bilibili in a vulnerable position after its exceptional run in the past two years. Gaming, like content, is vulnerable to AI, as we have seen the market aggressively discounting SaaS and gaming sectors. Bilibili is also at risk here. All in all, I am maintaining the stock at "hold".

Performance

Update: Q4 2025 Earnings

The recent earnings release by Bilibili was overall on par with expectations. The growth of advertisement revenue was especially robust, up by 27% in 4q25. However, the story was different for gaming and VAS. As mentioned in the previous thesis, BILI will likely not grow its user base significantly. To attract user growth, it may push for higher-quality content, which may compress margins due to the amount paid to content creators. Part of this has already been shown in 2025. Revenue-sharing costs vs. VAS revenue has been over 100% for all of FY25, higher than the previous two years.

Ever since 2H24, the drivers of margin have clearly been advertisement and gaming. Ad revenue per MAU in the recent quarter was CNY8.3, still having room to grow considering the level of peers and having efficiency tailwinds like AI. As for gaming, growth has remained a challenge ever since the release of San Mou in 3q24. There are some mentions of its pipeline, but that doesn't necessarily provide a significantly positive outlook on top of 10+% segment revenue growth expectation until FY27.

Recent Performance

The stock had performed sluggishly after the earnings release, coinciding with a broader sell-off for HK equities, especially for the tech sector. This seems reasonable while Bilibili's short-term outlook errs on the neutral-to-negative side. The market is concerned with the narrative of "AI eating software". This puts Bilibili on the spot, given its reliance on gaming to carry growth forward, and it is the area that hasn't seen any meaningful progress in the latest quarters. Momentum around the stock is slowly fading, which makes it vulnerable after its clear outperformance in 2024 (+70%) and 2025 (+25%).

Gaming sell-off

FY25 was a decent year overall, "marquee" as per management. Gross margin reached 37% vs. the 40-45% medium-term target. That said, margins have been at that level since 3q24, when the San Mou game was released. Another incremental 1% was likely attributed to the sustained growth in advertising efficiency and strong progress in converting general users to subscribers. Monthly paying users has reached 35 million in 4q25 vs. 29 million in 4q24. Overall, margin improvements were on par with expectations for 2025. The company finally began to operate at a profit with tailwinds from advertising and gaming, with advertising being the much more consistent one. That said, the company still trades at a 62x P/E. Moving forward, hitting the gross margin goal is the expectation.

Costs vs. revenue

Growth is challenged for both VAS and gaming. Even with the substantial growth of premium from subscribers, the broader VAS earnings do not share the same narrative, which may reflect pressure for content and the "take-rate" that Bilibili gets from these activities. QoQ earnings from gaming have been continuously slipping in 2025, even with a decent pipeline. This reflects the necessity to roll out games other than San Mou; otherwise, growth will continue to be pressured. Right now, the strategy is focused on international expansion while having 3 "major" pipelines of releases focusing on San Mou's more "hardcore" iterations.

Growth breakdown by segment

Final Take

Bilibili's new product story, primarily from gaming, has been fueling outstanding returns for more than a year and a half. While noting strong progress in functions like advertising, I am emphasizing gaming because it is the single most dominant driver of margins - simply looking at the differences before and after 3q24. In the recent 2 quarters, gaming revenues have also experienced considerable decline, despite management alluding to a strong pipeline ahead.

Let's look at what the market thinks. Bilibili's smooth uptrend has really been before the release of results for Q3 and Q4. After that, it has been quite cyclical, driven by momentum and the overall market condition. The recent three months of elevated volatility is a potential signal that the stock is taking a turn after significant outperformance in 2024 and 2025. Sticking to fundamentals, earnings reactions were dull for both of the quarters despite not underperforming expectations.

Fundamentally, I am neutral on the stock, as I have alluded to in my earlier article that I do not expect a significant upside for growth due to more MAUs. Generally, its targeted user base is quite limited. Any efforts to expand its user ecosystem would likely be challenging and costly. Given Bilibili's recent efforts to attract users and content creators, margins have been pressured through higher revenue-sharing costs. That effect would have been more clear had it not been for the strong performance of advertising. To me, it is a bit ironic given management's simultaneously promoting the prowess of AI while supporting the growth thesis of gaming and content.

Consensus Checking

In my previous article, I had a target of $28. I will keep that as it is, given its already optimistic trajectory of gross margin expansion. The targets are overall on par with sell-side analysts. One particular bullish call was from JPMorgan, where BILI was notched up to overweight from neutral and a target price of $35 from $27 (March 2026). The reasoning behind it was the potential yield from AI investments that will continue to support advertising profitability and help user engagement.

No doubt these are valid takes. My expectations for advertising revenue growth for FY26 and FY27 were about 10-15% YoY, noting that its ad revenue per user still lags behind big tech peers. As for VAS, AI and algorithmic improvements were not the only driver for monthly paying user conversion, in my opinion. It was also a result of higher revenue-sharing costs to generate better content. Overall, I believe the path to growth and margin expansion is not clear yet, with a contingency on gaming. Per 4q25 earnings call, the plan was to distribute some of the games internationally in 2026. More recently, Morgan Stanley had also updated its call, suggesting three new games in the pipeline with reasonable scale, with an updated target price of $31.

I wouldn't necessarily buy into this update. I think that the market has been expecting solid growth from gaming to begin with (~10-20% YoY growth until 2027). Bilibili's stock has been on a significant rise ever since management set its medium-term gross margin target at 40-45% in the beginning of 2025. There wasn't any viable path for that other than growing its gaming revenue. If the market repriced BILI significantly after that, then it effectively already assumed that gaming growth will be robust until FY27. The segment's revenue grew by 14% in FY25 following the release of San Mou in mid-2024. No doubt this game was a huge lift for revenue in the beginning, but eventually it normalized, especially in 2H25. Moving forward, the major driver will be from "San Wang", a more hardcore version of San Mou. All in all, MS expects FY27 revenue from gaming to be about CNY7.8 billion, nothing too outrageously different, and hence I am keeping my "hold" rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Investors Title Company: Strong Fundamentals, Litigation Overhangs, Hold For Now (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
When I last commented on Investors Title Company (ITIC) in August of 2025, I was especially upbeat over their generous dividends drawn from solid performance overall. Since then, annual reports have been released, and dividends have been issued together with recent promises of further issuance. A legal dispute has been attended to, judgement passed, and an appeal has been made. The developments have partly impacted my thesis, which I revise in this updated analysis. Title insurance is a structurally supply-constrained industry where underwriting discipline and agent networks, not demand forecasts, drive long-term profitability. Within that framework, Investors Title Company (hereafter ITIC) occupies a niche position as a conservative, high-return underwriter with a tightly controlled agency footprint. Recent financial reports have, however, dampened income, a development reflected in the reduced special dividend for 2025. Nonetheless, I revise my thesis to Hold.

Performance Review

ITIC issues residential and commercial title insurance, tax-deferred real property exchange services, and investment management and trust services to clients. This is done through subsidiaries, which are National Investors Title Insurance Company (NITIC), Investors Title Exchange Corporation (ITEC), Investors Title Accommodation Corporation (ITAC), and Investors Title Management Services (ITMS). As far as premiums are concerned, the largest volumes come from title insurance on “properties located in North Carolina, Texas, South Carolina, Georgia, and Florida.” The services offered are susceptible to business cycles, economic policy, and regulatory changes.

In prior coverage (mid-August 2025), ITIC traded near $230-$245/stock. Since then, the stock has appreciated to a high of $287.31 before sliding back to around $233.88 in April 2026. Financial reports for the 2025 financial year-end reveal a company enjoying healthy returns in terms of profitability. This is reflected in the gross margins, which show a gradually improving status (from 11.7% in 2023 to 16.3% in 2025). Operationally, the thesis has partially played out. FY2025 results showed revenue growth of 5.6% and net income rising to $35.2M ($18.57 EPS), supported by higher premiums and ancillary services. However, quarterly volatility persists, with Q4 earnings declining modestly year-over-year—a change that resonates with the cyclicity in the sector.

Revenue also shows signs of increasing concentration in Title Insurance, with the two major additional service areas lagging.

This concentration, together with geographic area concentration (85.5% of premium revenues came from five states), exposes ITIC to risk while reinforcing its stature within specific fields.

In March 2026, the company issued a dividend of $0.46/share, maintaining the dividend amount at a similar level to the previous issuance. The special dividend for 2025 ($8.72) was, however, significantly less than that issued in 2024 ($14.00).

Industry Context: The Title Insurance Capital Cycle

The title insurance industry is one of the most misunderstood corners of financial services. Unlike traditional insurance lines, losses are minimal; profitability is driven by pricing discipline and cost structure.

In June 2025, the title insurance industry’s top 5 underwriters controlled 73.1% of the market, while the top 10 controlled 88%. That is, this is an industry generally dominated by a few players across the country, and ITIC is not one of them. An additional feature of the industry is high regulatory barriers, largely in the form of state-level licensing. As a result, competition is stiff among participants already in the market, and little room exists for new entrants. This scenario augurs well for ITIC, which has a strong footprint in some states. However, it also implies that expanding into new geographic markets can transpire at a slow pace. Factors affecting expansion also include relationships with attorneys, lenders, and real estate professionals.

On the demand side, this is not a demand-driven industry in the conventional sense. Housing volumes fluctuate, but capacity, the number of underwriters and agents competing for business, determines pricing discipline.

ITIC Valuation: A Mispriced Earnings Stream

As of 10 April 2026, ITIC’s stock price was $233.88/stock, and earnings per share were $18.57 (TTM), implying that ITIC trades at approximately 12x earnings. It ranged between $210.13 and $279.27 YTD. In conducting a valuation analysis, I consider the upside/downside of current prices against four measures: the sector median, peers, Damodaran’s multiples, and a weighted average that considers the three. The peers that I consider include First American Finance Corporation (FAF), Fidelity National Financial, Inc. (FNF), Heritage Insurance Holdings, Inc. (HRTG), Kinsale Capital Group, Inc. (KNSL), and Kingstone Companies, Inc. (KINS).

The graphic below is derived from computations of the P/E figures, target prices (EPS x P/E), and Upside/Downside.

P/E Multiple Source

P/E

Target Price = EPS × P/E

Upside/(Downside)

Source: Seeking Alpha, Damodaran, and author calculations

The sector median suggests a target price of $245.50, indicating a modest upside of approximately 5%. In contrast, the peer group yields a lower implied value of $217.64, pointing to a downside of roughly 7%. A more optimistic framework produces a target of $335.19, implying substantial upside, though this outcome likely embeds assumptions around normalized earnings and favorable cycle conditions that may not yet be fully realized. An equally weighted blend of these approaches results in a target price of $266.11, or approximately 13.8% above current levels. Taken together, the valuation presents a balanced but inconclusive picture. While upside exists, it is neither uniform nor sufficiently robust across methodologies to establish a clear margin of safety. As such, the risk-reward profile appears broadly neutral at present levels.

This approach assumes no earnings growth, a conservative simplification. If earnings expand with housing normalization or margin improvement, upside could exceed the estimates. The quantitative picture, however, tells only part of the story. Several qualitative factors have a bearing on the investment case.

A Few Caveats

Some underlying concerns typically do not appear on balance sheets and income statements. Regarding ITIC, financial analyses do not highlight the extraneous yet pertinent matters that affect its operations and viability. Broadly categorized as risks, these have been acknowledged by ITIC in SEC filings, and one prominent risk is regulatory and litigatory.

In February 2025, Reginald Luster (the plaintiff) filed a lawsuit against ITIC (the defendant) claiming breach of contract. The matter was heard in the Georgia North District Court, and on September 12, judgement was passed in favor of the defendant. A notice of appeal was lodged on 17 September 2025 by the plaintiff, a move that initiated suspension of the judgement passed a few days prior. A notice of appeal was then issued on 19 September, and sitting on the matter is pending at the time of this investment update. The burden on ITIC is onerous in yet another legal dispute between the insurance firm and Cardinal Financial, wherein a $510,000 loan for a mortgage was granted by Cardinal to a fraudster. Cardinal sought to claim insurance cover for the full amount over the mortgage loan because ITIC was the designated underwriter. Although a ruling was made in ITIC’s favor on 09 February 2026, unresolved matters in the dispute are the issuance of a title policy as well as whether Cardinal’s claims of unfair trade practices and bad faith can be granted.

The two litigation processes are ongoing, meaning that time and resources continue to be expended in a matter that neither adds value nor creates stockholder wealth.

Equally notable is the cyclical nature of earnings growth, which is not sustainable, especially in an industry with little room for slippage. To show the cyclical nature of the business, I compiled the quarterly EPS for the past three years.

Q1 EPS has consistently been the weakest quarter across 2023-2025, with recovery typically emerging in Q2 and peaking in Q3.

A concerning development is the company’s stock rating change, which was downgraded to ‘Neutral’ in March 2026. This, however, is not to be confused with the earlier credit rating by AM Best—a global credit rating agency—in December 2025. The credit rating firm affirmed a Financial Strength Rating of A (Excellent), which indicates that ITIC is well capable of meeting ongoing financial obligations to policyholders.

Competitive Position: A Conservative Compounder

ITIC's competitive advantage is subtle but powerful, and arguably more durable precisely because it is not built on the kind of scale or technology advantages that attract imitation. A substantial portion of ITIC's premiums flows through its established underwriting subsidiaries and agent network, supporting both residential and commercial transaction volumes. Because agent commissions are variable, rising and falling with transaction volumes, the company avoids the overhead burden that crushes margins at larger, more branch-heavy competitors during downturns. The increase in operating expenses for 2025 was largely driven by agent commissions and other expenses corresponding to higher transaction volume, confirming the variable cost nature of the model. Revenue scales; the fixed cost base does not.

As of December 2025, the company’s investment portfolio, which underpins the insurer’s float, was well more than its reserve for claims. Both ITIC and NITIC met statutory premium reserve requirements as well as premium reserve requirements (and capital and surplus requirements) across every state where they are licensed.

Surplus cash, which has been a consistent feature on quarterly and annual balance sheets, continues to signal both capital adequacy and meaningful excess capital. Management has consistently deployed this surplus through regular quarterly dividends, special dividends, and buybacks rather than aggressive expansion. The company has returned nearly half of its current market cap via dividends over the last six years while growing book value per share at approximately 6% annualized.

Conclusion

ITIC is a well-run, conservatively financed title insurer with improving margins, a flexible cost structure, and a management team that has consistently returned capital to shareholders. The investment case is not broken; it is simply incomplete. Two litigation processes remain unresolved, the special dividend was meaningfully reduced, and valuation across my selected methodologies offers only modest upside at current prices. Housing market normalization would change that calculus, but that catalyst remains pending. For now, the stock is fairly priced for what it is: a high-quality compounder in a slow-moving industry, without a near-term trigger to justify a more aggressive position. Hold.

This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.
